--- SOURCE 2: Improving soil microbial inoculations in agricultural systems ---
URL: https://portal.nifa.usda.gov/web/crisprojectpages/1032638-improving-soil-microbial-inoculations-in-agricultural-systems.html

SUMMARY:
Grant No. 2024-67012-42839. Cumulative Award Amt. $225,000.00. Proposal No. 2023-09707.

FULL CONTENT:

| **Progress** 07/15/24 to 11/14/24  **Outputs** Target Audience:In the scientific aims of Dr. Custer's proposal, he will engage growers on the eastern shore of Maryland and Deleware. This area is home to many economically disadvantaged growers who would benefit from longer lasting benefits of bioinculuants. Beyond the scientific aims of his proposal, Dr. Custer is working to develop a set of outreach activities to engage disadvantagedsecondary school students and introduce the importance of soil microbes for promoting stress tolerance. This effort aligns with the third aim of Custer's proposal. At his new institution, Custer has fostered a relationship with Mr. Jerry Kelly, the director of Parkside High School's A+ Garden Centre. This program is the largest high school garden enterprise in the United States and is funded through the Wicomico Country Career and Technical Education program. Parkside High School is located in Salisbury, MD, and serves a mixture ofinner-city and rural students.As part of their certification, students are encouraged to engage in scholarly research to enhance horticultural productivity in the plant system of their choice. Custer has worked with his new institution to integrate two high school interns into this research program. They will begin working on the project in the spring of 2025. Changes/Problems:The only challengeso far was the result of accepting a tenure-track job. Moving to my new institution, coupled with the timeline of project funding,prevented me from implementing the greenhouse component of this research in the first summer. Due to the timeline of my move and when the award was approved, I could not plant wheat during this growing season. To begin this portion of the proposed research, I have scheduled greenhouse space for the spring of 2025. This is a temporary challenge thatwill be rectified during thespring 2025. What opportunities for training and professional development has the project provided?This fellowship has given Dr. Custer first-hand experience with project management, financial planning, and the required reporting. He is currently working to implement the proposed research at his new institution and has also worked through the process of transferring a grant between institutions. His continued work on this project will culminate with peer-reviewed publications, and his work with Mr. Kelly will facilitate continued outreach capacity in his lab group. The relationship with Mr. Kelly will promote training opportunities for local high school students. How have the results been disseminated to communities of interest? Nothing Reported What do you plan to do during the next reporting period to accomplish the goals? Nothing Reported   **Impacts** What was accomplished under these goals? Obj. 1 & 2 - Improving AMF and bacterial inoculations for enhanced phosphorous uptake by plants Custer ran preliminary trials to identify the rates for biological inoculant application in phosphorous-depleted soils. Along with an undergraduate student, Dr. Custer carried out an additional trial using the Arabidopsis thaliana Fro22 knockout to gain insight into the ability of phosphorous-solubilizing bacterial inoculants to alleviate iron stress. Preliminary results suggest that a single application of commercially available phosphorous-solubilizing bacteria at low rates can promote iron uptake. In addition, Dr. Custer has worked to isolate and identify the bacterial and fungal inoculants described in his proposal using molecular methods. Dr. Custer has worked with the greenhouse manager (Dr. Nadine Burton-Stubbs) at UMES Extension to schedule his first wheat planting for the spring of 2025 and the plants will run through the summer of 2026 in accordance with the adjusted project timeline. Obj. 3 - Outreach activity to increase the involvement of URMs in agroecology In this aim, Dr. Custer proposed to develop an outreach activity aimed at high school students. The goal of this activity was to increase high school student awareness of soil microbial ecology, agroecology, and soil sciences. While originally planned to be implemented in Pennsylvania, since his move to UMES, Custer worked with students from a local high school (Parkside High School, Salisbury, MD). This high school offers a certificate in Horticulture through its Career and Technology Education (CTE) program and runs the largest student-led greenhouse in the US (A+ Community Gardens, Salisbury, MD). The program director, Mr. Jerry Kelly, has opened his doors and allowed Custer to interact with his students several times. To date, he has lectured to two sections of Mr. Kelly's class, reaching nearly 30 students. At this time, there are additional plans to offer research internships to his students at UMES. Students will help Dr. Custer carry out the research proposed as part of this grant.   **Publications** |
|

**Progress** 07/15/24 to 11/14/24  
  
**Outputs**  
Target Audience:In the scientific aims of Dr. Custer's proposal, he will engage growers on the eastern shore of Maryland and Deleware. This area is home to many economically disadvantaged growers who would benefit from longer lasting benefits of bioinculuants. Beyond the scientific aims of his proposal, Dr. Custer is working to develop a set of outreach activities to engage disadvantagedsecondary school students and introduce the importance of soil microbes for promoting stress tolerance. This effort aligns with the third aim of Custer's proposal. At his new institution, Custer has fostered a relationship with Mr. Jerry Kelly, the director of Parkside High School's A+ Garden Centre. This program is the largest high school garden enterprise in the United States and is funded through the Wicomico Country Career and Technical Education program. Parkside High School is located in Salisbury, MD, and serves a mixture ofinner-city and rural students.As part of their certification, students are encouraged to engage in scholarly research to enhance horticultural productivity in the plant system of their choice. Custer has worked with his new institution to integrate two high school interns into this research program. They will begin working on the project in the spring of 2025. Moving to my new institution, coupled with the timeline of project funding,prevented me from implementing the greenhouse component of this research in the first summer. To begin this portion of the proposed research, I have scheduled greenhouse space for the spring of 2025. This is a temporary challenge thatwill be rectified during thespring 2025. **Impacts**  
What was accomplished under these goals?
Obj. 1 & 2 - Improving AMF and bacterial inoculations for enhanced phosphorous uptake by plants Custer ran preliminary trials to identify the rates for biological inoculant application in phosphorous-depleted soils. Along with an undergraduate student, Dr. Custer carried out an additional trial using the Arabidopsis thaliana Fro22 knockout to gain insight into the ability of phosphorous-solubilizing bacterial inoculants to alleviate iron stress. Preliminary results suggest that a single application of commercially available phosphorous-solubilizing bacteria at low rates can promote iron uptake. In addition, Dr. Custer has worked to isolate and identify the bacterial and fungal inoculants described in his proposal using molecular methods. Dr. Custer has worked with the greenhouse manager (Dr. Nadine Burton-Stubbs) at UMES Extension to schedule his first wheat planting for the spring of 2025 and the plants will run through the summer of 2026 in accordance with the adjusted project timeline. Obj. 3 - Outreach activity to increase the involvement of URMs in agroecology In this aim, Dr. Custer proposed to develop an outreach activity aimed at high school students. The goal of this activity was to increase high school student awareness of soil microbial ecology, agroecology, and soil sciences. While originally planned to be implemented in Pennsylvania, since his move to UMES, Custer worked with students from a local high school (Parkside High School, Salisbury, MD). This high school offers a certificate in Horticulture through its Career and Technology Education (CTE) program and runs the largest student-led greenhouse in the US (A+ Community Gardens, Salisbury, MD). The program director, Mr. Jerry Kelly, has opened his doors and allowed Custer to interact with his students several times. To date, he has lectured to two sections of Mr. Kelly's class, reaching nearly 30 students. At this time, there are additional plans to offer research internships to his students at UMES. Students will help Dr. Custer carry out the research proposed as part of this grant.

# Microbial Inoculant Treatments as an Alternative Spray for Disease Control that Reduces the Toxicity and Use of Copper in Organic and Sustainable Viticulture

### Project Overview

### [View the project final report](https://projects.sare.org/project-reports/fnc03-467/)

### Commodities

### Practices

### Summary:

BACKGROUND  
(Editors Note: The organic production of grapes in eastern U.S. is largely dependant on copper based fungicides for disease control. The copper has plenty of negative effects on the vines, the soil, on humans and on the environment in general as well as on equipment. Thus Ms. Patricia Iubelt sought to identify alternatives to the use of copper containing fungicides in a spray program for wine grapes grown in the east.)

--- SOURCE 5: Bio-Inoculants for Food Security in Africa - ICGEB ---
URL: https://www.icgeb.org/bio-inoculants-for-food-security-in-africa/

SUMMARY:
We provide funding opportunities to foster advanced education, to address scientific problems of regional interest and to promote cooperation.

FULL CONTENT:
![ICGEB](https://www.icgeb.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/ICGEBlogo-1.png)

[ICGEB](https://www.icgeb.org/)

International Centre for Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology

#### About us

An Intergovernmental Organisation with operations aligned to those of the United Nations Common System, we are a Centre of excellence for Research, Training and Technology Transfer to industry to promote sustainable global development.

#### Our research

With laboratories on three continents, our scientists work to advance basic research in the Life Sciences to improve Agriculture, Health, Education, and Energy Innovation. Our labs offer a scientific environment of top international standard, cutting-edge instrumentation, specialised facilities and advanced services.

#### Project Areas

We partner with Government bodies, academic institutes, private companies, and charities on projects to address hunger, health and well-being, quality education, affordable and clean energy, industry, innovation and infrastructure, all with a view to [gender parity](https://www.icgeb.org/women-in-science/), supporting [youth](https://www.icgeb.org/icgeb-alumni/), and Least Developed Countries.

#### Programmes

We provide funding opportunities to foster advanced education, to address scientific problems of regional interest and to promote cooperation.

#### ICGEB Search

Search here for anything you may be looking for that you don’t readily find.

#### Search in ICGEB site

# Bio-Inoculants for Food Security in Africa

![Government of Italy with ICGEB for Africa](https://www.icgeb.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Demis-Albertacci-photostudio-9-1.jpg)

ICGEB has received funding under the Mattei Plan for Africa: Italy’s strategic diplomacy, development cooperation and investment project to strengthen and renew ties with the Continent.

An ICGEB project to accelerate technology transfer to develop microbial bio-inoculant products for the agricultural and food sectors, suitable to the context of African countries has been funded by the Italian Government. The project promotes entrepreneurship, international collaborations, and the creation of new bottom-up initiatives.

The Mattei Plan for Africa is Italy’s strategic diplomacy, development cooperation, and investment project to strengthen and renew ties with the continent. The Plan includes developing new projects and active support for initiatives already underway, sharing with African states the phases of elaboration, definition, and implementation, to bring real added value to the local populations.

The Italian Government has identified several pilot countries (Egypt, Tunisia, Algeria, Morocco, Côte d’Ivoire, Mozambique, Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia, and Kenya) for the first phase for concrete and ready-to-implement actions along six sectors of intervention: health, education and training, agriculture, water, energy, and infrastructure.

Funded for 2 million euros by the Italian Ministry of Foreign Affairs and International Cooperation, the project’s duration is foreseen for the next three years and involves bringing 3 strategic projects from the laboratory scale to production scale and 8 innovative projects to accelerate laboratory concepts to pilot scale.

Training is envisaged for numerous Fellows, technology transfer on specific technologies, regulatory systems, and methods to ensure economic gain from implementing innovative technologies.

Accelerating Research & Development, the projects will enhance the sharing of ideas and development of local competencies for innovation and entrepreneurship.

Italian industries will be involved and will work to enhance knowledge and partnerships from the outset.

“This is a wonderful opportunity,” states Marianna Maculan, Chief of External Relations, “Our Member countries in Africa cover a total population of 946 million people (69% of the overall population of the continent). Initiatives previously supported by ICGEB on the African Continent and with the Italian and South African Governments, have created a solid technical basis to ensure a swift lift-off for the project.”

The SIS FVG Meeting for International Cooperation and Scientific Diplomacy, held at the Area Science Park on 26 June, was a great occasion to highlight the project.

![](https://www.icgeb.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Demis-Albertacci-photostudio-9-1.jpg)
![](https://www.icgeb.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/795112f9-3b2b-4edd-b170-e9c50fe335ca-scaled.jpg)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Increase funding for microbial inoculant research to enhance soil health, promote sustainable agriculture, and drive innovation in microbial applications through grants and partnerships.

enriched_points.point_title: Incentives for Farmers Adopting Sustainable Practices
enriched_points.expanded_content: Implement financial incentives and subsidies for farmers who adopt microbial inoculants and sustainable practices, facilitating a transition towards eco-friendly agriculture.
Launch campaigns to educate the public and farmers about the benefits of microbial inoculants, enhancing understanding of their role in improving soil health and crop yields.
